findings.description: parse() materializes the entire CSV via list(reader) and then builds a second full list of Record objects (plus the rows[1:] slice copy at line 41), roughly tripling peak memory on large uploads; iterate the reader lazily with header = next(reader) instead.

count_rows() loads the whole file into memory with readlines() just to count lines (per-line str object overhead can exceed the file size several-fold); use 'with open(path) as f: sum(1 for _ in f)' or chunked binary b'\n' counting for O(1) memory.
File opened without a context manager or explicit close, so the descriptor leaks on exception paths and non-refcounting runtimes (checklist category 6: resource leaks).

stream_valid() is fake streaming: it generator-wraps the fully materialized parse() result, so peak memory is identical to the non-streaming path and the Iterator return type is misleading; make parse() yield rows lazily to get actual bounded-memory streaming.
The header index dict is computed on every parse() call and never used — dead per-call work, negligible cost but pure waste.

Rows are parsed by hard-coded position row[0..3] while the computed header index is ignored, so a CSV with valid-but-reordered columns passes the REQUIRED_COLUMNS check yet silently mis-parses (or raises IndexError on short rows) — brief note only, detail deferred to critic-logic.
count_rows() returns -1 for an empty file and miscounts logical CSV records when quoted fields contain embedded newlines — deferred to critic-logic.

find_by_id() is an O(n) linear scan per call, so any caller looping over targets degrades to O(n*m); if repeated lookups are expected, build a {id: record} dict once — speculative since no callers are in scope.
